question: Positronium (Ps) is a pure QED bound state of $e^+e^-$. What are its key spectroscopic features?

options: Ground-state binding 6.8 eV ($= R_y/2$, reduced mass); para-Ps singlet decays $\to 2\gamma$ (0.125 ns); ortho-Ps triplet decays $\to 3\gamma$ (142 ns); hyperfine 203 GHz between them
Positronium is identical to hydrogen with no observable differences in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
$e^+e^-$ cannot form a bound state due to Coulomb repulsion in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
Positronium has no annihilation channels and is stable indefinitely in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
passive.explanation: Positronium: reduced mass $m_e/2$, so binding $E_1 = -\alpha^2 m_ec^2/4 = -6.8$ eV (half hydrogen's). C-parity: photon has $C = -1$; even-$n$-photon final states have $C = +1$ (matches para-Ps $^1S_0$), odd-$n$ have $C = -1$ (matches ortho-Ps $^3S_1$). Decay channels selected by C-parity conservation. Lifetimes: para 125 ps to $2\gamma$; ortho 142 ns to $3\gamma$ ($\alpha$ extra factor). Used to test bound-state QED to high orders.

active.prompt: Match each pure-QED bound state to its properties:

pairs.definition: Binding 6.8 eV; lifetime 125 ps (para) or 142 ns (ortho); pure QED, no nuclear structure
Muon plays role of nucleus; binding 13.5 eV; lifetime $\sim 2.2\,\mu$s (muon decay); pure QED + small weak/hadronic
Muon bound to proton; orbit $\sim 200\times$ closer than $e$; samples proton structure; gives best $r_p$ measurement
Familiar atom; small finite-nuclear-size correction; benchmark for QED + bound-state methods

active.explanation: Each system tests different aspects of QED. Ps: pure QED at field-theory level. Muonium: pure QED with leptonic nucleus. Muonic-H: QED + proton structure. Hydrogen: highest-precision QED + nuclear corrections. Together they constrain QED parameters to 10-12 digits.

question: Para-positronium decay $p$-Ps $\to 2\gamma$ tests C-parity selection rules. What's the angular distribution?

options: Isotropic in the para-Ps rest frame (spin-0); two back-to-back photons each with $E = m_ec^2 = 511$ keV; polarisations correlated (entangled)
Anisotropic with $\sin^2\theta$ dependence in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
Photons have arbitrary energies summing to $2m_ec^2$ in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
Decay forbidden by parity regardless of the choice of regularisation scheme used in the calculation
passive.explanation: $^1S_0 \to 2\gamma$: spin-0 state, isotropic emission. Each photon: 511 keV (rest-frame energy from $E_\gamma = m_ec^2$). Back-to-back from momentum conservation. Linear polarisations of the two photons are orthogonal (from selection rules) — entangled photon state. Used in original Bell-test-type experiments (Wu-Shaknov 1950) testing nonlocality. Modern: PET (positron emission tomography) detects coincident 511 keV photons to localise positron annihilation in patient body.

question: Muonium ($\mu^+e^-$) is a pure leptonic bound state. Why is it interesting for testing QED?

options: Bound state of two leptons → no hadronic corrections; muon mass known precisely; tests bound-state QED with $\alpha^7$ accuracy; hyperfine measured to $10^{-9}$ precision
Muonium is exotic and very rare; barely studied experimentally in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
Has same lifetime as hydrogen (essentially infinite) in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
Equivalent to muonic hydrogen and provides no independent test in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
passive.explanation:
Muonium ($\mu^+e^-$): muon acts as 'heavy electron' nucleus. No nuclear structure → cleanest bound-state QED test. Lifetime: limited by muon decay $\mu^+ 	o e^+
u_e ar
u_\mu$ ($	au_\mu = 2.2\,\mu$s). Hyperfine splitting: measured $\Delta
u_{m Mu} = 4\,463\,302.776(51)$ Hz (LAMPF/PSI); theory matches at $10^{-9}$. Provides measurement of $m_\mu/m_e$ ratio and best test of QED bound-state methods (no proton-radius uncertainty).

question: Anti-hydrogen ($\bar pe^+$) was first synthesised at CERN and has been used for fundamental tests. What's the goal?

options: Compare H and $\bar{\rm H}$ spectra (test CPT theorem): expected identical by CPT, any difference $\Rightarrow$ CPT violation; also test gravitational behaviour of antimatter
Anti-hydrogen is used as a fuel source for matter-antimatter propulsion in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
$\bar{\rm H}$ has different chemistry from H and is used to make new molecules in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
Anti-hydrogen creates new chemical compounds with conventional matter in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
passive.explanation: CERN's ALPHA, ATRAP, AEgIS, GBAR experiments synthesise and trap $\bar{\rm H}$. CPT theorem predicts H and $\bar{\rm H}$ have identical spectra. ALPHA (2017): measured $\bar{\rm H}$ 1S-2S transition agreeing with H at 2 ppt. ALPHA (2018): measured Lamb shift in $\bar{\rm H}$. AEgIS, GBAR: test gravitational free-fall of antimatter (no anomaly found so far). Any difference between H and $\bar{\rm H}$ would shake fundamental physics (CPT violation, equivalence principle violation).

question: Highly charged ions (HCI, e.g. U$^{91+}$) probe QED in extreme strong-field regimes. Why are they important?

options: Effective coupling $Z\alpha$ approaches unity for $Z \sim 1/\alpha \sim 137$; strong-field QED corrections grow as $(Z\alpha)^n$; tests QED beyond perturbation in $Z\alpha$
HCI are easier to produce than neutral atoms; this result follows directly from the gauge-fixing prescription at hand
QED corrections vanish for high-$Z$ ions with no further corrections at any loop order in the perturbative expansion
Useful only for nuclear physics, not for QED testing in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
passive.explanation: Hydrogenic uranium U$^{91+}$ has electron orbiting Z=92 proton. Effective $Z\alpha \approx 0.67$ — strong-field. QED corrections: leading $\alpha(Z\alpha)^4 m_e c^2 \sim 60$ eV (much larger than hydrogen's 4×10⁻⁶ eV scale). $g - 2$ of bound electron: differs from free $g - 2$ by $\sim (Z\alpha)^2$. GSI, MPIK Heidelberg facilities produce and measure HCI. Tests non-perturbative bound-state QED. EBIT (electron-beam ion trap) is the main lab tool.

question: The Casimir effect is the attractive force between uncharged conducting plates from QED vacuum fluctuations. What's the formula?

options: $F/A = -\pi^2\hbar c/(240 d^4)$ attractive; geometric (no charge needed); confirmed experimentally by Lamoreaux 1997; arises from boundary conditions on EM modes
$F/A = +\pi^2\hbar c/(240 d^4)$ — repulsive force between plates in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
Casimir force is finite only in 1D; 3D gives infinite force in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
Force depends on plate charge density, not separation in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
passive.explanation: Casimir (1948): conducting plates exclude long-wavelength EM modes from the cavity; vacuum energy between plates is less than outside; net attractive force. Magnitude tiny but measurable. Lamoreaux (1997): first precision measurement with torsion pendulum. Modern: confirmed by many groups using AFM techniques. Has practical consequences for MEMS at nm scales. The Casimir-Polder version: force between an atom and a wall. Stark Casimir effect: vacuum-fluctuation effects also exist for fermion fields in cavities.

question: Strong-field QED (laser fields approaching the Schwinger limit) is a frontier. What characterises it?

options: Schwinger critical field $E_S = m_e^2 c^3/(e\hbar) \approx 1.3\times 10^{18}$ V/m; above it, vacuum spontaneously pair-produces $e^+e^-$; perturbation in $E/E_S$ breaks down; tested at ELI/XFEL/Apollon facilities
Strong-field QED is identical to ordinary perturbative QED at any field strength in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
Schwinger limit is $10^{30}$ V/m, far above laboratory capabilities in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
Above the Schwinger limit, QED predictions become trivially zero; this result follows directly from the gauge-fixing prescription at hand
passive.explanation: Schwinger limit: $E_S \sim 1.32 \times 10^{18}$ V/m or intensity $\sim 4.6 \times 10^{29}$ W/cm². Current laser intensities: $\sim 10^{23}$ W/cm² (Apollon, ELI under commissioning); approaching but not yet at Schwinger. Multi-petawatt lasers will probe regime where electrons can interact non-perturbatively with photons (multi-photon scattering, vacuum pair production). E144 experiment at SLAC (1990s): nonlinear Compton scattering observed with $\sim 10$ photons per interaction. Future: XCELS, Apollon, ELI extreme-light facilities aim at $10^{25}$ W/cm² and beyond.
